The **Ukrainian Cup** ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Кубок України [[ˈkubok ʊkrɐˈjinɪ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Ukrainian "Help:IPA/Ukrainian")) is an [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") national knockout cup competition run by the [Ukrainian Association of Football](/wiki/Ukrainian_Association_of_Football "Ukrainian Association of Football"). The competition is conducted almost exclusively among professional clubs.[[1]](#cite_note-prof-1) Since the 2003–04 season, the Cup winner qualifies to play the [Ukrainian Premier League](/wiki/Ukrainian_Premier_League "Ukrainian Premier League") winner for the [Ukrainian Super Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Super_Cup "Ukrainian Super Cup").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/69/Ukrainian_Cup.svg/250px-Ukrainian_Cup.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ukrainian_Cup.svg)

Old logo

## Current format and eligibility criteria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Cup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Current format and eligibility criteria")]

### 2025–26 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Cup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2025–26 season")]

Further information: [2025–26 Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Ukrainian_Cup "2025–26 Ukrainian Cup")

The competition includes all professional first teams from the [Premier League](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2024–25 Ukrainian Premier League") (16/16 teams of the league), [First League](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Ukrainian_First_League "2024–25 Ukrainian First League") (16/16), [Second League](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Ukrainian_Second_League "2024–25 Ukrainian Second League") (15/23) as well as 21 amateur teams including 5 best performers from the previous year's [Amateur Cup](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Ukrainian_Amateur_Cup "2024–25 Ukrainian Amateur Cup") and 16 from regional associations.

|  | | Teams entering this round | Teams advancing from the previous round |
| Qualification round (16 teams) | | 16 entrants from regional associations[[2]](#cite_note-sport-2) |  |
| Round of 64 (56 teams) | | 12 entrants of the [Premier League](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2025–26 Ukrainian Premier League")[[2]](#cite_note-sport-2) 16 entrants of the [First League](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Ukrainian_First_League "2025–26 Ukrainian First League")[[2]](#cite_note-sport-2) 15 entrants of the [Second League](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Ukrainian_Second_League "2025–26 Ukrainian Second League")[[2]](#cite_note-sport-2) 5 entrants of the [2024–25 Amateur Cup](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Ukrainian_Amateur_Cup "2024–25 Ukrainian Amateur Cup")[[2]](#cite_note-sport-2) | 8 winners from the Qualification round |
| Round of 32 (16 teams) | | 4 entrants from the [Premier League](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2025–26 Ukrainian Premier League")[[2]](#cite_note-sport-2) | 28 winners from the Round of 64 |

### Qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Cup&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Qualification")]

For the competition are eligible first teams of all Ukrainian professional clubs including the top tier, the Premier League, and lower tiers from the [Professional Football League](/wiki/Professional_Football_League_of_Ukraine "Professional Football League of Ukraine"), the First League (Persha) and the Second League (Druha). No reserve teams or second teams may enter the competition. An exception may be granted by the [Ukrainian Association of Football](/wiki/Ukrainian_Association_of_Football "Ukrainian Association of Football") if such team won the [Ukrainian Amateur Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Amateur_Cup "Ukrainian Amateur Cup") or other qualification tournaments.

Beside professional clubs, to the competition is also invited both finalists of the [Ukrainian Amateur Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Amateur_Cup "Ukrainian Amateur Cup") from the preceding season. If one or both finalists obtained professional status (admitted to the Second League (tier 3)), then one or more better performers of the amateur competition are invited, such as semifinalists or others.

The number of participants fluctuates from season to season around 50-70. This includes 30+ teams from the top two tiers, the Premier and the First leagues and about the same amount or less from the Second league and/or amateur participants. During the existence of the [Ukrainian Second League Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Second_League_Cup "Ukrainian Second League Cup") in 1999-2001, the number of participants was around 30. Following the 2014 Russian military aggression, the number of participants has sunk notably to around 50.

### Format: draw and team entry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Cup&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Format: draw and team entry")]

The format of this competition consists of two phases: a qualification phase with 2-3 rounds followed by the competition proper (3-4 rounds including the final game) when all Premier League (tier 1) clubs enter the competition.[[8]](#cite_note-cup_16-17-8)

The competition features a staggered entrance format where early rounds of the competition include matches between lower league competitions with teams of higher league competitions entering later. Often times the very first qualification round involves matches between the amateur teams and either the newly admitted professional clubs or clubs that struggled in prior season. After that in the next couple of qualification rounds enter clubs of the Second League (tier 3) and First League (tier 2).

Beside the initial draw, all the draws are conducted the next day after all the matches of the round is played. The draw for each round may be "blind" or teams may be grouped as "seeded" and "unseeded".

Often the [Ukrainian Association of Football](/wiki/Ukrainian_Association_of_Football "Ukrainian Association of Football") organizes the draw in qualification phase by geographic principles, so to accommodate "smaller" clubs (in lower tiers) by reducing their travel time.

### Timeframe and the final

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Cup&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Timeframe and the final")]

The competition usually starts in the early August or the second half of July. It takes an extensive break for winter months starting in November and resumes no early than the second half of March or early April. This break is driven by the climate situation.

The final takes place in the mid-May, often times, or by the end of May, normally.

Typically, the final used to take place at the [Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex](/wiki/Olimpiyskiy_National_Sports_Complex "Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex") in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), the country's capital, however due reconstruction of the stadium to prepare to the [UEFA Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"), the final had been played at other venues temporarily. After that this tradition has ceased.

### Past variations of the format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Cup&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Past variations of the format")]

Past variations of the competition involved a home-away type of elimination, but the Ukrainian Cup has since changed to a single game per round format. In recent years, a conditional replay game was introduced to avoid penalty shootouts. Cup draws may be conducted for two consecutive rounds, but usually occur before each following round. The lower division teams are usually awarded the home-field advantage (or the first leg at home in case of a two-leg round).

Until the big football reform of 1996, the competition featured winners of regional football cup competitions from all oblasts (regions) as well as the [Autonomous Republic of Crimea](/wiki/Autonomous_Republic_of_Crimea "Autonomous Republic of Crimea"). Following the 1996 reform, the qualification of the regional football cup competition winners was discontinued.

Between 1999 and 2001, additional competition existed, serving as a qualification for the Ukrainian Cup. It was known as the [Ukrainian Second League Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Second_League_Cup "Ukrainian Second League Cup"). It was created in order to make the Ukrainian Cup more competitive, involving participation of only the strongest teams. Following the discontinuation of the competition, the participation of the second teams (reserve teams) in the Ukrainian Cup became restricted.

## Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Cup&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Trophy")]

There were at least 4 trophies, 2 during the Soviet period and 2 following the [fall of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Soviet_Union "Fall of the Soviet Union").

The newest trophy was introduced right before the start of the [2025–26 Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Ukrainian_Cup "2025–26 Ukrainian Cup") competition, featuring the ceremonial mace "Bulava".

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Cup&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [Cup of the Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Cup_of_the_Ukrainian_SSR "Cup of the Ukrainian SSR")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Covers_of_the_SU_-_First_Ukrainian_SSR_Cup_1938.jpg/250px-Covers_of_the_SU_-_First_Ukrainian_SSR_Cup_1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:Covers_of_the_SU_-_First_Ukrainian_SSR_Cup_1938.jpg)

First Ukrainian SSR Cup on cover of the Soviet Union

Ukrainian Cup competitions have been conducted since at least 1936.[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) The first of season in 1936 was officially known as **Spring Championship**, the decision about which was adopted by the All-Ukrainian football Section.[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) Initially called also as the Spring Championship, sometime during the 1937 season the tournament was renamed by mass media as the **Cup of the Ukrainian SSR** ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Кубок УРСР, Kubok URSR).[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) The official change was adopted by the Republican Football Conference only in April 1938.[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) To commemorate the event, in 1979 the Soviet [Ministry of Communication](/wiki/Ministry_of_Communications_(Soviet_Union) "Ministry of Communications (Soviet Union)") released an envelope with depiction of the trophy (see the picture).[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) The streamer on top of a picture
reads in Russian language "The first Cup of Ukraine in football" ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Первый кубок Украины по футболу, Pervyi kubok Ukrainy po futbolu), while the same thing is written at the picture's footer in Ukrainian language ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Перший кубок України з футболу, Pershyi kubok Ukrayiny z futbolu).

In 1944 as compensation for the canceled republican championship there was conducted next tournament in September.[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) The decision to conduct the tournament was adopted on 6 September 1944 by the Central Committee of the Communist Party of Ukraine.[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) The tournament was also known as **Ukrainian Cup**[[10]](#cite_note-10) or Ukrainian Bowl ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Келих УРСР, Kelykh URSR).[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) After World War II, subsequent editions of the national Cup were downgraded to a republican cup competition that was limited to lower league clubs and teams participating in the KFK competitions (amateurs). The timeframe of the tournament also shifted from spring time to fall (end of calendar year).[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) Already in 1948 [FC Lokomotyv Kharkiv](/wiki/FC_Lokomotyv_Kharkiv "FC Lokomotyv Kharkiv") as one of the Soviet Top League clubs from Ukraine chose not to participate in the Ukrainian Cup competition.[[9]](#cite_note-cupussr-9) In 1959 the tournament was cancelled completely and replaced with Football Cup among collective of physical culture (a predecessor to [Ukrainian Amateur Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Amateur_Cup "Ukrainian Amateur Cup")).

In the 1970s, the Ukrainian Cup competitions were revived and conducted parallel to Ukrainian Amateur Cup for several seasons. In second half of 1970, the tournament was discontinued once again until 1990.

The first Cup competition in independent Ukraine had an unlikely winner, similar to the [championship of 1992](/wiki/1992_Ukrainian_Premier_League "1992 Ukrainian Premier League"). The main contender, [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv"), settled for a draw in its first game at home against a team that was an amateur club in Soviet times, [Skala Stryi](/wiki/FC_Hazovyk-Skala_Stryi "FC Hazovyk-Skala Stryi"). In the following quarter-finals round, the team faced defeat by [Torpedo Zaporizhia](/wiki/FC_Torpedo_Zaporizhia "FC Torpedo Zaporizhia"). Eventually that competition was won by [Chornomorets Odesa](/wiki/FC_Chornomorets_Odesa "FC Chornomorets Odesa").

In 2008, the Football Federation of Ukraine signed a contract with the company Datagroup,[[11]](#cite_note-11) naming the company as the main sponsor of the tournament for the next four years. Datagroup introduced its new version of the cup trophy,[[12]](#cite_note-12) the first winner of which became [Shakhtar Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar Donetsk").[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2010, there was an attempt to launch an independent website for the competition, which was active for only a couple of months.
